front: Ballet: features piercing F-sharp eighth notes in the xylophone and flute, followed by loud trombone glissandi from F down to D
Ballet: set on a collective farm and ends with a triple wedding
Ballet: the title character's husband Giko burns down a cotton warehouse and attempts to kill her; Kazakov nurses her back to health and marries her
Ballet: adapted from an earlier ballet titled Happiness
Ballet: folk instruments like the daira drum feature in this work
Ballet: the Sabre Dance is based on a folk song from this country's musical tradition
Ballet: the Sabre Dance's middle section features cellos and saxophone playing a sinuous melody opening with rising pickup quarter notes F, A-flat, B-flat landing on B-natural
Ballet: the triple wedding couples include Karen and Nune, and Kazakov and the title character
Ballet: contains an adagio depicting carpet weavers
Ballet: the adagio from this work was used in the score of 2001: A Space Odyssey
Ballet: Giko threatens to throw his child Ripsime off a cliff
Ballet: in the Sabre Dance, a cymbal clash interrupts one of the A section's repetitions
Ballet: the Sabre Dance features a loud timpani ostinato that alternates beats with the orchestra
Ballet: the Act Two Adagio depicts the escape of the title character and his wife Phrygia from captivity
Ballet: the Adagio from this work is often performed separately as a standalone concert piece
Work: opens with a waltz in A minor, often described as grim
Work: incidental music to Mikhail Lermontov's play, extracted into a five-movement suite consisting of waltz, nocturne, mazurka, romance, and galop
Work: ends with a romance and a galop; includes an E-flat major mazurka with triplets on the downbeat
Concerto: violin concerto written for and premiered by David Oistrakh
Concerto: first movement marked Allegro con fermezza; opens with the soloist playing two sixteenth-note Ds, then eighth-notes F-E-F
Concerto: this violin concerto unusually calls for tambourine and a piccolo snare drum
Concerto: this violin concerto was transcribed into a flute concerto by Jean-Pierre Rampal
Concerto: second movement opens with a mournful bassoon solo repeated an octave higher by a clarinet solo; features a jittery E-flat, F-flat, D-flat, E-flat motif
Concerto: includes a duet between clarinet and soloist in the second cadenza of the Allegro con fermezza first movement
Piano: virtuosic toccata written in the rare key of E-flat minor
Piano: toccata written while studying under Myaskovsky, popularized by classmate Lev Oborin
Piano: this toccata vastly overshadows the remainder of the suite for which it was originally written
Piano: features a central Andante espressivo episode between outer Allegro marcatissimo sections; repeats four-beat rhythmic units accelerating from quarter notes to sixteenth notes
Symphony: opens with the piercing motif E, G-sharp, F; nicknamed "The Bell"
Symphony: uses a bell to both open and close this work
Symphony: the composer swapped the order of the Allegro risoluto and Andante sostenuto middle movements when revising this work
Symphony: contains the Dies Irae at half speed on strings, in a movement based on a folk song about a mother learning of her son's death
Symphony: written in honor of the twenty-fifth anniversary of the Russian Revolution
Concerto: piano concerto in D-flat major written for Lev Oborin; calls for a musical saw (flexatone)
Concerto: performances of this piano concerto made pianist William Kapell famous
Concerto: this composer wrote concertos over ten years for each member of a renowned piano trio (Oborin, Oistrakh, and Knushevitsky)
Symphony: single-movement work calling for 15 trumpets and a solo organ; subtitled "Symphony-Poem"
Composer: studied under Reinhold Gliere and Nikolai Myaskovsky
Composer: targeted in the 1948 Zhdanov decree for formalism, along with Shostakovich, Prokofiev, and Myaskovsky
Composer: his Cello Concerto was criticized by the Soviet government for Western intrusions like quoting the Dies Irae
Composer: wrote two Combat Marches for brass band
